title: Painting over a dark colour on interior walls
subtitle: Get a clean, opaque finish when switching from a deep or vivid paint
excerpt: How to paint over a dark or vivid colour on interior walls and achieve an even, streak-free finish in the new colour. Covers why dark colours bleed through, the tinted primer approach, and the minimum coat count needed.
sourceNotes: British Standard BS 6150: Code of practice for painting of buildings. Dulux Technical Helpline guidance (publicly available). Collins Complete DIY Manual, Albert Jackson and David Day (1989).

glossaryTerms.definition: A diluted first coat of emulsion paint (roughly 10 to 20% water added) applied to a porous surface or as a thin sealing coat before full coats. On bare plaster it reduces suction. When covering dark paint, a tinted mist coat can serve as an intermediate neutralising coat between the dark base and the final colour.

A primer formulated to block stains and dark colours from bleeding through a new topcoat. Usually tinted grey or mid-tone to reduce the number of topcoats needed when painting a lighter colour over a darker one. Available in trade paint ranges and some consumer brands as a base coat specifically for colour changes.

aliases: painting light colour over dark colour

content.text: Painting a light colour over a dark one requires more coats than painting over a similar tone. Dark pigments (particularly deep reds, blues, and greens) bleed through subsequent coats for weeks if not sealed first. Applying white emulsion directly over a dark colour without an intermediate coat is the most common mistake: the result is a streaky, uneven finish that requires five or six coats instead of two.

in a mid-grey or mid-tone tint. Final topcoat emulsion in the desired colour. A 9-inch roller with a medium-pile sleeve (10 to 12 mm). A 50 mm brush for cutting in. A paint tray. Masking tape. A sanding block with 120-grit paper for light keying between coats.
Step 1, wash and key the existing surface

Wash the existing painted surface with warm water and a sugar soap solution to remove grease and dust. Allow to dry. Lightly sand with 120-grit paper to key the surface; this helps the primer grip rather than sliding on the glassy surface of emulsion paint. Wipe off dust with a just-damp cloth. Do not wet the surface before painting.
Step 2, apply the tinted primer coat

Apply the tinted primer with the roller, cutting in along edges and corners with the 50 mm brush first. The primer is usually a grey or warm neutral; its job is to reduce the contrast between the dark base and the light topcoat, not to look good. Apply in a thin, even coat. Do not try to obliterate the dark colour in one pass: one good primer coat reduces it enough for the topcoat to do its job cleanly. Allow the primer to dry fully; 2 to 4 hours depending on the manufacturer.
Step 3, apply the topcoat; minimum two coats
Apply the first topcoat once the primer is dry. Roll in a light, even pass and allow to dry for the time stated on the tin (usually 2 hours for water-based emulsions). Apply the

approach only on bare plaster, not between topcoats on painted surfaces. On painted surfaces, apply each coat full-strength. Two topcoats over the primer achieves a fully opaque, even finish in almost all cases. A very dark red or black may need a third topcoat.

If the new colour is close in tone to the original (repainting a dark blue in a dark green, for example), a primer is not needed. Two full coats of the new colour will cover. Bleed-through only occurs when a dark colour sits under a much lighter one. If the existing paint is gloss rather than emulsion, sand it first. Use a primer made for adhesion over gloss.

attrs.symptom: Dark colour still showing through after two topcoats
attrs.cause: No intermediate primer was used, or the primer was too thin.
attrs.fix: Apply a coat of tinted obliterating primer at this stage (it will work on top of the existing topcoats), allow to dry, then apply a further topcoat.

attrs.symptom: Uneven sheen with patches of different finish on the same wall
attrs.cause: The undercoat was not fully dry before the topcoat was applied, causing the topcoat to sink into some areas more than others.
attrs.fix: Allow a full 4 hours between coats. In cold or damp rooms, allow overnight before the next coat.
